2604.25905.title: A paradox of AI fluency
2604.25905.paper_summary: How much does a user's skill with AI shape what AI actually delivers for them? This question is critical for users, AI product builders, and society at large, but it remains underexplored. Using a richly annotated sample of 27K transcripts from WildChat-4.8M, we show that fluent users take on more complex tasks than novices and adopt a fundamentally different interactional mode: they iterate collaboratively with the AI, refining goals and critically assessing outputs, whereas novices take a passive stance. These differences lead to a paradox of AI fluency: fluent users experience more failures than novices -- but their failures tend to be visible (a direct consequence of their engagement), they are more likely to lead to partial recovery, and they occur alongside greater success on complex tasks. Novices, by contrast, more often experience invisible failures: conversations that appear to end successfully but in fact miss the mark. Taken together, these results reframe what success with AI depends on. Individuals should adopt a stance of active engagement rather than passive acceptance. AI product builders should recognize that they are designing not just model behavior but user behavior; encouraging deep engagement, rather than friction-free experiences, will lead to more success overall. Our code and data are available at https://github.com/bigspinai/bigspin-fluency-outcomes
2604.25905.paper_summary_zh: 使用者對 AI 的技能在多大程度上影響 AI 實際為他們提供的結果？這個問題對使用者、AI 產品建設者以及整個社會來說都至關重要，但仍然未被充分探討。<lb/>通過使用來自 WildChat-4.8M 的 27K 轉錄本的豐富註釋樣本，我們顯示流利的使用者承擔比新手更複雜的任務，並採用根本不同的互動模式：他們與 AI 進行協作迭代，精煉目標並批判性地評估輸出，而新手則採取被動的姿態。<lb/>這些差異導致了 AI 流利度的悖論：流利的使用者經歷的失敗比新手更多——但他們的失敗往往是可見的（這是他們參與的直接結果），更可能導致部分恢復，並且伴隨著在複雜任務上的更大成功。<lb/>相比之下，新手更常經歷不可見的失敗：看似成功結束的對話實際上卻未能達到目標。<lb/>綜合這些結果，重新框定了成功使用 AI 所依賴的因素。<lb/>個體應該採取主動參與的姿態，而不是被動接受。<lb/>AI 產品建設者應該認識到，他們設計的不僅僅是模型行為，還是使用者行為；鼓勵深入參與，而不是無摩擦的體驗，將導致整體上更多的成功。<lb/>我們的代碼和數據可在 https://github.com/bigspinai/bigspin-fluency-outcomes 獲得。
2604.25905.author: Christopher Potts et.al.
